content:
# Numbers Chapter 33 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 190-192 | **Source Image:** `NUM_033_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_033_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_033_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0139-pa_0140

---

**1** These are the stages of the journey of the sons of Israel who came out of the land of Egypt by their divisions, under the hand of Moses and Aaron.

**2** And Moses recorded their starting points, stage by stage, at the command of YHWH. And these are their stages according to their starting points:

**3** And they set out from Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day of the first month — on the day after Passover the sons of Israel marched out with a raised hand, in full view of all Egypt.

**4** And the Egyptians were burying those whom YHWH had struck down among them — every firstborn — for YHWH had executed judgments against their gods.

**5** And the sons of Israel set out from Rameses and camped at Succoth.

**6** And they set out from Succoth and camped at Etham, which is on the edge of the wilderness.

**7** And they set out from Etham and turned back toward Pi-hahiroth, which faces Baal-zephon, and they camped before Migdol.

**8** And they set out from before Hahiroth and crossed through the middle of the sea into the wilderness, and they traveled three days' journey into the wilderness of Etham and camped at Marah.

**9** And they set out from Marah and came to Elim. And at Elim there were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they camped there.

**10** And they set out from Elim and camped by the Sea of Reeds.

**11** And they set out from the Sea of Reeds and camped in the wilderness of Sin.

**12** And they set out from the wilderness of Sin and camped at Dophkah.

**13** And they set out from Dophkah and camped at Alush.

**14** And they set out from Alush and camped at Rephidim, and there was no water there for the people to drink.

**15** And they set out from Rephidim and camped in the wilderness of Sinai.

**16** And they set out from the wilderness of Sinai and camped at Kibroth-hattaavah.

**17** And they set out from Kibroth-hattaavah and camped at Hazeroth.

**18** And they set out from Hazeroth and camped at Rithmah.

**19** And they set out from Rithmah and camped at Rimmon-perez.

**20** And they set out from Rimmon-perez and camped at Libnah.

**21** And they set out from Libnah and camped at Rissah.

**22** And they set out from Rissah and camped at Kehelathah.

**23** And they set out from Kehelathah and camped at Mount Shepher.

**24** And they set out from Mount Shepher and camped at Haradah.

**25** And they set out from Haradah and camped at Makheloth.

**26** And they set out from Makheloth and camped at Tahath.

**27** And they set out from Tahath and camped at Terah.

**28** And they set out from Terah and camped at Mithkah.

**29** And they set out from Mithkah and camped at Hashmonah.

**30** And they set out from Hashmonah and camped at Moseroth.

**31** And they set out from Moseroth and camped at Bene-jaakan.

**32** And they set out from Bene-jaakan and camped at Hor-haggidgad.

**33** And they set out from Hor-haggidgad and camped at Jotbathah.

**34** And they set out from Jotbathah and camped at Abronah.

**35** And they set out from Abronah and camped at Ezion-geber.

**36** And they set out from Ezion-geber and camped in the wilderness of Zin — that is, Kadesh.

**37** And they set out from Kadesh and camped at Mount Hor, on the border of the land of Edom.

**38** And Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at the command of YHWH, and he died there in the fortieth year after the sons of Israel had come out of the land of Egypt, in the fifth month, on the first day of the month.

**39** And Aaron was one hundred and twenty-three years old when he died on Mount Hor.

**40** And the Canaanite king of Arad — who dwelt in the Negev, in the land of Canaan — heard of the coming of the sons of Israel.

**41** And they set out from Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah.

**42** And they set out from Zalmonah and camped at Punon.

**43** And they set out from Punon and camped at Oboth.

**44** And they set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, on the border of Moab.

**45** And they set out from Iyim and camped at Dibon-gad.

**46** And they set out from Dibon-gad and camped at Almon-diblathaim.

**47** And they set out from Almon-diblathaim and camped in the mountains of Abarim, before Nebo.

**48** And they set out from the mountains of Abarim and camped on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho.

**49** And they camped by the Jordan, from Beth-jeshimoth as far as Abel-shittim, on the plains of Moab.

**50** And YHWH spoke to Moses on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho, saying:

**51** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you cross over the Jordan into the land of Canaan,

**52** then you shall drive out all the inhabitants of the land from before you, and you shall destroy all their carved images, and all their cast metal figures you shall destroy, and all their high places you shall demolish.

**53** And you shall take possession of the land and settle in it, for I have given the land to you to possess it.

**54** And you shall divide the land by lot as an inheritance among your clans. To the larger group you shall give a larger inheritance, and to the smaller group you shall give a smaller inheritance. Wherever the lot falls for each person, that shall be his. You shall receive your inheritance according to the tribes of your fathers.

**55** But if you do not drive out the inhabitants of the land from before you, then those you allow to remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides, and they will harass you in the land where you are dwelling.

**56** And what I had planned to do to them, I will do to you."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "stages" (מַסְעֵי / mas'ey):** The Hebrew noun derives from the root נסע (nasa', "to pull up, to set out"), referring to the pulling up of tent pegs to break camp. Each "stage" is literally a "pulling-up" — a departure point. The entire chapter is structured as a travel log, a theological geography of wandering.

**Verse 3 — "with a raised hand" (בְּיָד רָמָה):** This vivid idiom conveys boldness, defiance, and triumph. The sons of Israel did not slink away — they marched out openly, defiantly, under the gaze of all Egypt. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this χειρὶ ὑψηλῇ, preserving the same image.

**Verse 4 — "executed judgments against their gods":** The Hebrew מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim) means "acts of justice." YHWH's defeat of Egypt is framed not merely as rescue but as a legal verdict — a divine court ruling against the Egyptian pantheon itself. The plagues were prosecutions.

**Verse 8 — "crossed through the middle of the sea":** The Hebrew בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם emphasizes passage *through the interior* of the sea, not merely alongside it. This is the core exodus miracle recalled in compressed form within the itinerary.

**Verse 9 — "twelve springs...seventy palm trees":** The numbers are symbolically loaded: twelve corresponds to the tribes, seventy to the elders (or the traditional count of nations). Elim is portrayed as a place of complete provision.

**Verse 14 — "no water there for the people to drink":** A terse editorial note breaking the monotone rhythm of the itinerary. The crisis at Rephidim (cf. Exodus 17) is recalled with characteristic understatement.

**Verses 18-37 — The wilderness stations:** Many of these place names are otherwise unknown and appear only in this itinerary. Some are etymologically transparent: Kibroth-hattaavah ("graves of craving"), Hazeroth ("enclosures"), Rithmah (possibly from רֹתֶם, "broom plant"), Rimmon-perez ("pomegranate of the breach"). The list functions as a compressed memorial — forty years reduced to a catalog of encampments.

**Verse 38 — Aaron's death:** The itinerary pauses to record Aaron's death with unusual precision: the fortieth year, fifth month, first day. This is one of only two dates given for an individual death in the Torah (the other being Moses in Deuteronomy 34). The detail dignifies the moment within the relentless forward movement of the journey.

**Verse 39 — "one hundred and twenty-three years old":** Aaron's age at death anchors him in the genealogical timeline. He was three years older than Moses (cf. Exodus 7:7).

**Verse 40 — The king of Arad:** This parenthetical notice breaks the itinerary pattern entirely. The Canaanite king's awareness of Israel's approach foreshadows the conquest narrative about to unfold. The Vaticanus text preserves this notice in the same position.

**Verse 52 — "carved images...cast metal figures...high places":** Three categories of Canaanite religious infrastructure are targeted: מַשְׂכִּיֹּת (maskiyyot, "image-stones" or carved cult objects), צַלְמֵי מַסֵּכֹת (tsalmey massekot, "molten images"), and בָּמוֹת (bamot, "high places" — elevated cultic platforms). The command is comprehensive: the religious landscape must be entirely cleared.

**Verse 55 — "barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides":** The Hebrew שִׂכִּים (sikkím, "barbs/pricks") and צְנִינִם (tseniním, "thorns") create a vivid bodily metaphor. Incomplete obedience will result in perpetual irritation — not a distant threat but an intimate, daily affliction. This warning echoes Joshua 23:13 and Judges 2:3.

**Verse 56 — "what I had planned to do to them, I will do to you":** The chapter ends with a stark reversal: the judgment intended for Canaan's inhabitants will fall on Israel if they fail in their task. The verb דִּמִּיתִי (dimmiti, "I imagined/planned") carries a sense of predetermined design — YHWH's intentions are not arbitrary but purposeful, and they are transferable.

**Structural note:** Numbers 33 is unique in the Torah as a systematic travel log. Its rhythmic, repetitive cadence ("And they set out...and they camped") creates a liturgical quality — the reading of the list itself becomes an act of remembrance. The chapter bridges the wilderness narrative (vv. 1-49) and the conquest mandate (vv. 50-56), positioning Israel at the threshold of the land.
